description: This neuron detects the first letter or initial character of a proper noun, company name, location, or title at the beginning of a text segment or document.
The neuron detects the beginning of a new line or content section immediately following a line break, often at the start of code blocks, copyright notices, or article text.
This neuron detects line breaks or newline characters, particularly when they appear at the beginning of a new line or after markup/code comment symbols.
The neuron appears to detect closing XML/HTML tags or the beginning of tag closures, particularly when they appear at the start of a line or after whitespace.
This neuron detects the word "divisor" when it appears in mathematical contexts asking to calculate the greatest/highest common divisor (GCD) of two numbers.
This neuron detects decimal points in numerical values and periods in file extensions, as well as newline characters in code snippets.
This neuron detects colons that introduce or precede code blocks, quoted text, or explanatory content, particularly in technical documentation and programming contexts.
The neuron detects the dash or hyphen character used as a separator in compound terms, file paths, or between related elements in technical documentation.
This neuron detects line breaks or paragraph breaks, particularly those that separate distinct sections of text or introduce new topics.

This neuron detects the beginning of XML/HTML closing tags or attribute values, specifically firing on the opening character of multi-character sequences like ">>" or when a ">" precedes another structural element.

This neuron detects contractions and auxiliary verbs (particularly "don't", "wasn't", "is") as well as line breaks or transitions in text, often appearing before a clarifying or contrasting statement.

This neuron detects newline characters that occur immediately after certain opening or delimiting tokens (such as angle brackets, colons, braces, or "do" keywords), typically indicating the start of a new line in structured text like code, HTML, or Q&A formatting.
This neuron detects periods that end academic or technical abstracts, typically occurring after the introduction or background section and before the methodology or detailed explanation begins.
This neuron detects period tokens that mark the end of a title, heading, or introductory phrase before transitioning to the main body text or new section.
The neuron appears to detect periods (dots) used as package/namespace separators in programming languages (particularly Java) and version numbers, as well as sentence-ending periods in regular text.
The neuron appears to detect line breaks or newline characters that occur at the end of code comments, legal/official document headers, or copyright notices.
This neuron detects periods that end sentences, particularly in formal or technical writing contexts such as academic papers, patents, news articles, and corporate documents.
This neuron detects periods that mark the end of a sentence or clause, particularly when followed by a new sentence beginning with a capitalized word or proper noun.
This neuron detects sentence-ending periods that conclude formal or technical statements, particularly in academic, scientific, or professional contexts.
The neuron appears to detect Japanese hiragana characters and particles within Japanese text, particularly in the context of explaining Japanese phrases or grammar structures.
This neuron detects sentence-ending periods that conclude academic or scientific article titles, particularly in medical and biological research contexts.

This neuron detects words that complete idiomatic phrases or common expressions, particularly those indicating conclusion, limitation, or conventional usage (e.g., "works like a charm," "to say the least," "of its day," "limited time only").
This neuron detects periods that end sentences in academic or scientific text, particularly in titles, abstracts, or introductory sections of research papers.
This neuron detects periods that mark the end of academic or technical article titles, typically preceding the start of an abstract or article body.

This neuron detects capitalized words at the beginning of titles, headings, or sections, particularly common discourse markers and organizational terms like "How," "Work," "Why," "Abstract," and "Comparison."

This neuron detects abstract nouns denoting non-physical concepts or roles, particularly those related to quantities, positions, categories, or social relationships (such as "wake," "hands," "request," "dose," "part," "types," "amount," "leadership").
The neuron detects capitalized words at the beginning of XML/PHP declarations, HTML-like tags, or standalone capitalized words that serve as labels, headings, or category markers.
This neuron detects capitalized words or proper nouns that appear at the beginning of titles, headings, or topic labels, often indicating the start of a new section or subject matter.
This neuron detects the equals sign "=" appearing as an HTML attribute assignment operator within reference link markup, specifically in the "ref-type=" attribute of citation hyperlinks.
This neuron detects periods that mark the end of technical or scientific paper titles, particularly those that appear before the abstract or introduction section begins.
